coredata.dc:description: Ethylene glycol (EG) is a widely used material, but its vapor is harmful to human health already in low concentrations. Thus, highly sensitive EG gas sensors are urgently needed. Herein, a series samples of ZnO and carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized at different ratios through a simple mechanical grinding method. The senor made using ZnO/CDs2 exhibits an ultra-high response (Ra/Rg) to 100 ppm EG up to 2798, 1378 and 467, and extremely low detection limit as low as 21 ppb, 13 ppb and 2 ppb, under UV light, visible light, and infrared light, respectively, as well as excellent repeatability and long-term stability. Especially, under UV light irradiation, the response of ZnO/CDs2 to 100 ppm EG is 24 times that of pure ZnO (Ra/Rg = 114.7), and more than 71 times that of 9 other gases. The outstanding gas sensing performance of ZnO/CDs2 can be attributed to the excellent light response ability of CDs at first, which greatly enriches the electron concentration in ZnO/CDs under light irradiation. Furthermore, the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) property of CDs and the p-n heterojunction formed at the interface between ZnO and CDs play a key role in rapid electron transport and transfer, avoiding a large number of electron hole recombination.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ethylene glycol (EG) is a widely used material, but its vapor is harmful to human health already in low concentrations. Thus, highly sensitive EG gas sensors are urgently needed. Herein, a series samples of ZnO and carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized at different ratios through a simple mechanical grinding method. The senor made using ZnO/CDs2 exhibits an ultra-high response (Ra/Rg) to 100 ppm EG up to 2798, 1378 and 467, and extremely low detection limit as low as 21 ppb, 13 ppb and 2 ppb, under UV light, visible light, and infrared light, respectively, as well as excellent repeatability and long-term stability. Especially, under UV light irradiation, the response of ZnO/CDs2 to 100 ppm EG is 24 times that of pure ZnO (Ra/Rg = 114.7), and more than 71 times that of 9 other gases. The outstanding gas sensing performance of ZnO/CDs2 can be attributed to the excellent light response ability of CDs at first, which greatly enriches the electron concentration in ZnO/CDs under light irradiation. Furthermore, the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) property of CDs and the p-n heterojunction formed at the interface between ZnO and CDs play a key role in rapid electron transport and transfer, avoiding a large number of electron hole recombination.

ce:section.ce:para: Ethylene glycol (EG) is a typical volatile organic compound (VOC). Due to highly reactive chemical properties, EG is often used as an important solvent in various fields, such as the production of antifreeze and coolants, as well as in the textile, plastic, and coating industries [1–3]. However, EG vapor has a relatively high toxicity to mammals, roughly equivalent to that of methanol, with a human olfactory threshold concentration of 4.33 ppm. Excessive inhalation of EG vapor can lead to serious consequences, such as respiratory distress, coma, renal failure, pulmonary edema, central nervous system dysfunction, and even death. According to the guidelines of the American Academy of Clinical Toxicology regarding the treatment of EG poisoning, the time-weighted average tolerable concentration (PC-TWA) of EG is 7.2 ppm, and the short-term tolerable exposure concentration (PC-STEL) is 14.4 ppm [4]. Based on this, in practical applications, sensitive detection of EG vapor as low as 7.2 ppm is required to ensure human health, and sensitive detection of EG vapor as low as 4.33 ppm is required to ensure that people are not affected by odors.

Gas sensors have been widely used to detect different gases in various industries due to their low cost, miniaturization, integration, and real-time detection [5,6]. As an important type of gas sensing material, metal oxide semiconductors (MOS) with various crystal structures exhibit outstanding sensing performance [7]. Among them, ZnO is the most widely used n-type MOS material due to its wide band gap, non-toxicity, good thermal and chemical stability, and high electron mobility [8–10]. There are many modification strategies used to improve the gas sensing performance of ZnO, such as morphological modulation, surface modification of noble metals, doping with rare earth elements and the preparation of multi-component composites [11–15]. The carbon nanomaterials are excellent candidates for composite with MOS due to low-cost, easy to prepare, and flexible surface state [16,17] For example, Ding et al. prepared the ZnO/reduced graphene oxide (rGO) nanosheets by combining chemical precipitation and hydrothermal method. Compared with intrinsic ZnO gas sensor, the operating temperature of ZnO/rGO is reduced and its response is increased by 1.1 times [1]. The work of Yu et al. also demonstrated that the gas sensing response of ZnO/carbon quantum dot composite materials to NO gas is significantly higher than that of ZnO microspheres [18]. Additionally, Park et al. present a highly responsive ppb-level methanol gas sensor device using exfoliated graphene sheets and ZnO quantum dots on an aluminum anodic oxide template (AAO). The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the high surface area graphene sheets and ZnO with a 3.37 eV bandgap efficiently absorbing UV light led to 56 times high response due to the enhanced polarization on the sensor surface compared to non-UV-radiated EGs/AAO at 800 ppb of methanol. The limit of detection concentration is obtained at 2 ppb level [19]. Among numerous performance improvement strategies, it is an effective way to use light to excite photo-generated electrons, thereby enriching the electron concentration of the sensitive layer. This significantly enhances the response signal of gases. Thereby lower operating temperatures can be used. In the past decade, light-activated gas sensors have attracted more and more attention [16,17,20]. Due to the large band gap of metal oxides, UV light was the most widely used form of light. For instance, Wang et al. prepared mesoporous ZnO nanosheets for CH4 gas sensing. Under UV irradiation, ZnO nanosheets show a response from 62 % to 0.1 % to CH4, which is about 4.7 times higher than commercial ZnO nanoparticles [21]. Liu et al. constructed the heterogeneous interface electric field by loading In2O3 nanoparticles onto ZnO nanorods for higher separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers, stronger response signals, and faster response/recovery times. The results showed that at room temperature, 10 mol% In2O3 modified ZnO (In: Zn) exhibits a response of 117.0 (Rg/Ra) to 700 ppb NO2 under UV light irradiation, with a response time of 100 s and a recovery time of 31 s [22]. However, the high energy of UV light may cause photo-aging of devices and photo-bleaching of gas sensitive materials. Therefore, multicolor light should be attempted to be selected. For example, wang et al. synthesized ultra-thin ZnO nanorods (approximately 15 nm) using a nano assisted wet chemistry method, and subsequently functionalized them with Au nanoparticles through photoreduction. Due to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of Au nanoparticles, hybrid materials exhibit visible light activity. The ZnO/Au hybrid was assembled into a high-performance photo controlled gas sensor that operates at room temperature. The sensor has stronger selectivity for NH3 in the dark, but high selectivity for NO2 under visible light irradiation (λ = 532 nm) [23].

In this study, carbon dots (CDs) are selected due to their excellent fluorescence properties and unique photo-induced electron transfer (PET) properties [24,25]. The n-type ZnO and p-type CDs were combined by mechanical grinding to prepare ZnO/CDs sensors for the detection of EG. We analyzed the effect of the introduction of CDs on the amount of oxygen vacancies (Ov) of ZnO/CDs through XPS, and then studied the PET property and light response ability of CDs contributed to electron concentration through fluorescence spectra and photocurrent measurements. Furthermore, the gas sensing performance of ZnO and ZnO/CDs under UV light, visible light, infrared light and in the dark was systematically tested. It was found that compared with pure ZnO, under UV light, the ZnO/CDs2 gas sensor exhibits higher response signals (Ra/Rg = 2798) and faster response/recovery times (45 s/9 s). More importantly, the gas sensing mechanism of ZnO/CDs under light and no light were compared and analyzed, respectively. This work sheds light on the design of high-performance gas sensors and light excitation sensors.

Zinc chloride (ZnCl2), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent. Urea (H2NCONH2), sodium alginate ((C6H7O6Na)n), and ethanol (CH3CH2OH) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents involved in our experiments were of analytical purity and used directly without further purification. The chemicals for the gas sensitivity tests, ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2), isopropanol (C3H8O), ethanol (C2H5OH), formaldehyde (CH2O), methanol (CH3OH), acetone (C3H6O), n-butanol (C4H10O), n-propanol (n-C3H8O), ammonia (NH3·H2O), formic acid (HCOOH) and α-Terpineol (C10H18O) were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The purity of the liquids was analytical pure (≥99.7 %), except for α-Terpineol with a purity of 98 %. The used formaldehyde solution has a concentration of 37 %.

First 0.68 g of ZnCl2 and 0.12 g of PVP were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water at a molar ratio of 400:1. After mechanical stirring for 30 min, 2 g (0.05 M) of NaOH were added to the above solution and vigorously stirred for 1 h. The solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor and kept in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. After that, the solid reaction product was repeatedly cleaned and centrifuged with ethanol and deionized water. The powder obtained by centrifugation was dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The dried powder was put into a mortar for grinding, and then put into a muffle furnace, heated to 500 °C at a rate of 2 °C/min in air for 2 h. The white powder obtained was the prepared ZnO.

Amounts of 2 g sodium alginate and 1 g urea were ground up. The mixture was then transferred to an alumina crucible and heated for 5 h at 200 °C in air atmosphere in an oven [28]. After cooling to room temperature the sample was taken out of the crucible and ground again. The black powder obtained was dissolved in deionized water. The reddish brown solution obtained was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles, and then filtered. The filtrate obtained was dialysis for 48 h with a 10000 Da dialysis membrane. After freeze-drying the brown powder obtained was CDs.

The ZnO/CDs samples for sensor investigations were prepared by a mixed mechanical grinding method. 0.1 mg of CDs were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water to form a pale yellow solution. 500 μL, 1000 μL, and 1500 μL solution were dropped into 1 g ZnO powder using a pipette and fully mixed and ground in a mortar for 3 min thereby forming the ZnO/CDs1, ZnO/CDs2 and ZnO/CDs3 samples, respectively. The sample without CDs was pure ZnO. Fig. 1 gives a schematic view of the complete materials preparation process.

The crystalline phases of samples were identified by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, D/max-2200, Japan) with the measuring range from 5° to 80° (2θ). The radiation used was CuKα with a wavelength of 0.154 nm, the current and voltage of the generator were 40 mA and 40 kV, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab) was used to analyze the chemical structure of samples. Monochromatic AlKα radiation was used with an energy of 1486.6 eV. The spot size was 400 μm, the working voltage 12 kV, and the filament current 6 mA. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, 200cx, Japan) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM, JEOL, 2100F, Japan) were used to investigate the morphology of samples. A fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, F-7000) was used to record photoluminescence (PL) spectra of samples. A UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu 1700) measured the band gap of samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and measurements of gas sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Amounts of 1 mg of the sensor powders were placed in a mortar, along with an appropriate amount of turpentine alcohol and ethanol and ground for 1 h to form a homogenous paste. The paste was then uniformly distributed with a scraper on the surface of hollow Al2O3 ceramic tubes to form a film of appropriate thickness, which was dried in an oven at 60 °C (Fig. S1). Four Pt wires were fixed on the surface of the Al2O3 ceramic tube. Two pairs of these Pt wires were welded to a gold electrode on the ceramic tube, which form the sensor base. After that, a Ni–Cr alloy heating wire was inserted in the middle of the ceramic tube and welded to the sensor base. This arrangement was inserted into a test circuit board and placed on an aging table at 220 °C for continuous aging for 2 days to improve the stability of gas sensor. The sensor performances were tested by the measurement system (WS-30A, Winsen Electronics Technology Co., Ltd). Working voltage was 5 V, and the working temperature was changed by adjusting the heating voltage, load cards with different resistances played the role in adjusting the voltage divider. The injection-type static gas distribution method was utilized under the environment with the temperature of 25 °C and the humidity of 40 % RH, the environmental humidity was adjusted by using different saturated salt aqueous solutions [15]. In addition, the powers of used infrared light and UV light sources both were 300W, and the distance between light source and sensors was 30 cm. The response value “S” of the sensor was defined as the ration of Ra/Rg (S= Ra/Rg), where Ra and Rg refer to the resistance values of the sensor in air and target gas, respectively. Meanwhile, the response and recovery time refer to the time the sensor resistance reaching 90 % of the equilibrium state during gas injection and release.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of ZnO and ZnO/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the structure and morphology of the samples, a series of measurements was carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 a-b, the prepared ZnO forms monodisperse micrometer sized flowers with the particle size of 5–10 μm. They consist of sharp edged columns with lengths of about 5 μm and a width of about 2 μm. After composite with CD, there was no significant change in the morphology of ZnO (Fig. 2c-d). Fig. 2e and Fig. S2 exhibit the XRD patterns of ZnO, ZnO/CDs and CDs, respectively. All characteristic diffraction peaks matched those of ZnO as given in PDF#89–1397. This shows that the prepared ZnO micrometer sized flower crystals have the hexagonal wurtzite structure. The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 31.73°, 34.38°, 36.21°, 47.48°, 63.77°, and 67.86° correspond to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) crystal planes of ZnO [26]. There are no other impurity peaks, implying that ZnO is phase pure. The broad diffraction peaks at 2θ = 20°–30° contributed by the CDs can be observed in the patterns of the ZnO/CDs [18]. Moreover, with the increase of CDs content, the intensity of the broad diffraction peaks increases.

The elemental analysis shows that Zn, O, and C are uniformly distributed in ZnO/CDs1 and ZnO/CDs2 and thereby that CDs are successfully loaded into the ZnO micro-flowers (Fig. 3 a-b). Fig. 3c shows the HRTEM image of ZnO/CDs2. It is clearly observed that the CDs are distributed within the ZnO uniformly. The measured crystal plane spacing of ZnO is 0.241 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of hexagonal fibrous ZnO [27]. Fig. 3d shows the SAED patterns of typical ZnO single crystals, demonstrating good crystallinity of ZnO micro flowers. A more detailed view of the morphology of CDs is shown in Fig. 3e. The particle size of the CDs is between 2 and 7 nm, and the line spacing, which correspond to the (100) crystal plane of graphene, is 0.21 nm [28].

The surface elements and chemical states of ZnO and ZnO/CDs were analyzed by XPS. Fig. S3a shows the full XPS spectra of ZnO, CDs and ZnO/CDs2. The N peaks of ZnO and ZnO/CDs2 are derived from PVP. The C peak of ZnO/CDs2 is derived from amorphous carbon and CDs [19]. After that, the high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum of CDs exhibits characteristic peaks of C–O and O–C=O at 532.1 and 536.3 eV, respectively (Fig. S3b). The N1s signal of CDs is subjected to peak fitting as shown in Fig. S3c. The N1s peak can be divided into N–H and N–C at 401.9 and 399.7 eV, respectively. In addition, the C1s signal of CDs exhibits the characteristic peaks of C–C/C=C, C–O–C/C–OH, and O–C=O at 284.8, 286.1, and 288.4 eV, respectively (Fig. S3d) [29]. Compared with pure ZnO, a new peak appears in the C1s spectrum of ZnO/CDs2, which is the O–C=O peak located at 286.1 eV, caused by CDs (Figs. S3(e–f)).The FTIR spectra of ZnO, CDs, and ZnO/CDs are shown in the SI, Fig. S4. The absorption peak at 1084 cm−1 and 550 cm−1 of ZnO can be attributed to the Zn–O–Zn bond and stretching vibration of the Zn–O bond, respectively [8,10]. The strong absorption peak at 3414 cm−1 corresponds to an –OH bond. The absorption peaks at 2079, 1580, 1348, and 1049 cm−1 are related to C–H bending and C=O and C–O–C bond vibrations of PVP. Compared to pure ZnO, ZnO/CDs2 exhibits significant C=O peaks at 1580 and 1348 cm−1, what is consistent with the C1s spectral characterization results of the XPS measurements.

The defect engineering of ZnO is one of the strategies to improve the gas sensing properties, especially for oxygen defects [30]. Fig. 4 a shows two symmetric peaks in the Zn 2p range of the spectrum of both ZnO and ZnO/CDs, proving that the zinc ion is a Zn2+ single type unit. The peak centered at 1020 eV corresponds to Zn 2p3/2, and that at 1044 eV corresponds to Zn 2p1/2. The distance between the two peaks is about 23.0 eV, which is consistent with reported data [21]. The binding energy of Zn in ZnO/CDs shifts to the low energy compared to that of pure ZnO. The reason is that if the number of bonded O ions decreases, the charge transfer from Zn to O will decrease, thereby increasing the shielding effect of Zn valence band electrons and reducing the electron binding energy of Zn ions [31]. As seen in Fig. 4b-e, the high-resolution spectrum of O1s can be fitted to three major peaks at around 529, 531, and 532 eV, corresponding to lattice oxygen (OL), surface active oxygen vacancies (OV), and surface adsorbed oxygen (OC), respectively. Among them, OV can provide active sites for gas adsorption and reactions on the surface of sensing materials, which can play an indispensable role in high performance sensors [32]. The OV contents of ZnO, ZnO/CDs1, ZnO/CDs2, and ZnO/CDs3 are 31.99 %, 34.89 %, 40.20 %, and 30.46 %, respectively (Fig. 4f). With the addition of CDs, the OV content increases firstly. With larger amounts of CDs the content of OV decreases again. The reason is that when CDs and ZnO form the composite through grinding in a mortar, functional groups on the surface of CDs can draw O from the surface of ZnO to form oxygen vacancies (Ov) [35]. What's more, as the ratio of CDs composites increases, more and more O were taken away from the surface of ZnO, and the content of Ov increases. However, during the preparation process, we found that the CDs exhibited significant aggregation induced quenching effects [29]. Therefore, when CDs are excessively complexed, CDs will lose their unique properties (electron donor/acceptor) due to the local aggregation, which is not conducive to capturing O from ZnO, leading to a sharp decrease in Ov. Among them, the ZnO/CDs2 sample has the largest amount of OV, with about 26 % more than the pure ZnO.

Furthermore, EPR spectra shown that the peak at 3500 G of ZnO/CDs2 is stronger than that of the other three samples, indicating that ZnO/CDs composites can produce more OV on the materials surface, which is consistent with the XPS results (Fig. 5 a). The OV optimize the energy level fine tuning and produce more adsorption sites and therby enhance the response of the gas sensors. The four samples exhibit a similar peak at about g = 2.003, which is thought to be the oxygen species (O2 −/O−) absorbed in the sample Ov sites. The strong ZnO/CDs2 signal implies that there are more adsorption sites (Fig. 5b) [26].

The excellent light response ability of CDs can effectively improve the photoelectric performance of ZnO. As shown in Fig. 6 a, pure ZnO can emit red fluorescence, with an emission peak around 630 nm. When the excitation wavelength changes, the emission peak position of ZnO is almost unchanged, indicating that ZnO has fixed emission centers. Similarly, CDs emit red fluorescence fixed at 605 nm and thereby have also an excitation wavelength independence property (Fig. S5). With the increase of the CDs recombination ratio, both the fluorescence emission peak intensity and shape of the ZnO/CDs samples change compared to pure ZnO. The fluorescence emission intensity of ZnO/CDs has been significantly weakened compared to pure ZnO, indicating a decrease in the photo-generated electron hole recombination rate of ZnO/CDs (Fig. S6). It means that the CDs with PET capability can effectively reduce the electron hole recombination rate and further enrich the photo-generated electron concentration in ZnO/CDs. In Fig. 6(b–d) it can be seen that the fluorescence emission peak shapes of ZnO/CDs are asymmetric, suggesting that they have more than one emission center. In order to compare in detail the changes of various luminescent centers of ZnO after the introduction of CDs, the fluorescence emission peaks of ZnO and ZnO/CDs were fitted with three calculated emission lines under 300 nm excitation, with peaks at approximately 555 nm, 620 nm, and 640 nm, respectively (Fig. 6e). The peak emission at 555 nm is caused by the singly ionized oxygen vacancy (VO +), whereas that at 620 nm is related to the doubly ionized oxygen vacancy (VO 2+), The emission peak centered at 640 nm may be due to oxygen interstitials (Oi) [22,23,33]. Compared to pure ZnO, the proportion of emission peak intensities from OV (VO ++VO 2+) in ZnO/CDs significantly increases. More OV can provide more active sites to promote the gas sensing process. Among them, ZnO/CDs2 has the highest content of OV, indicating its optimal gas sensing performance.

Changes in optical behavior are often caused by changes in optical band gaps. As shown in Fig. 7 and Fig. S7, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, ZnO and ZnO/CDs2 are accurately measured. The optical energy band gap was determined by the Tau method [34]. The incident photon energy (hν) and the absorption coefficient (α) have the following mathematical relationship:
(1) E = hν = hc/λ
(2) (αhν)1/m = B(hν − E
g )
Where λ denotes the maximum wavelength (nm), h is the Planck's constant, c is the speed of light in the band attributed to the π-π* leap, B is a constant, E
g is the energy band gap of the material, and the index m depends on the type of transition. Fig. 7a-d and Fig. S7b shows the (αhν)2 versus hν plot, which reflects the direct optical bandgap of materials. The optical bandgap of pure ZnO is 3.11 eV. The optical bandgap of CDs is 2.28 eV. After the introduction of CDs, the optical bandgap of ZnO/CDs narrows to 2.95 eV. The reason is that the p-n heterojunction is formed at the interface between ZnO and CDs, which can induce band bending, manifested macroscopically as a change in the band gap of ZnO/CDs. The narrower optical bandgap means that light of lower energy can excite photo-generated electrons, leading to electron transition and transfer.

Afterwards, the photo-current characteristics of ZnO and ZnO/CDs were further tested, as shown in Fig. 8 a. During 6 cycles, the reproducibility of the photocurrent density of all samples is very well, indicating that they have stable optoelectronic properties. Excellent repeatability and reproducibility are key performance indicators of the sensitive materials to be used for manufacturing light-excited gas sensors [35,36]. The photocurrent density of the four samples rapidly increases at the beginning of each light illumination. After turning off the light, the photocurrent density rapidly decreases. This can suggest that the four samples respond quickly to light and have short-life photo-generated electrons, which is conducive to achieve rapid detection of target molecules. In fact, the higher the photocurrent density, the stronger the photoelectric conversion ability, the more abundant are the photo-generated charge carriers in the materials. Obviously, with the increase of CDs recombination ratio, the photocurrent density of ZnO/CDs first increases and then decreases. The photocurrent density of ZnO/CDs2 is the highest, which is 3.4 times that of pure ZnO. It is because that the excellent light response ability of CDs greatly enriches the concentration of photo-generated electrons in ZnO/CDs2, and their unique PET property can effectively transfer electrons to avoid a large number of electron hole recombination. It is an effective strategy to improve the gas sensing performance of materials by increasing electron concentration. Therefore, compared to pure ZnO, ZnO/CDs2 is predicted to have better gas sensing performance. The EIS (Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy) measurement was used to quantitatively analyze the resistance characteristics of ZnO and ZnO/CDs (Fig. 8b). Usually, the smaller radius of the Nyquist curve corresponds to the faster electron transfer kinetics and lower charge transfer resistance of the redox reaction. The diameter of the Nyquist plot of ZnO/CDs decreases with the increase of the amount of CDs. It indicates that compared to ZnO, ZnO/CDs have better charge transfer ability and can greatly promote charge transfer. The stronger the photocurrent, the smaller the impedance, which is consistent with many literature reports [34]. It further confirms that CDs can improve electron transfer ability and increase the photocurrent density of ZnO.

The gas sensing performance of ZnO and ZnO/CDs was systematically analyzed in Fig. 9 . Among them, the ZnO/CDs2 sensor with richest OV and best photoelectric response ability has been given special attention. Both ZnO and ZnO/CDs sensors have a significant response to EG gas at 140–300 °C (Fig. 9a). As the temperature increases, the response signals of the four sensors first increase and then weaken. The reason is that the gasification temperature of EG is about 197.85 °C. When the operating temperature of the sensors is relatively low, EG is not active and does not have enough activation energy to react with the oxygen species adsorbed on the surface of the material, resulting in a low gas response. As the working temperature increases, the oxygen adsorbed on the surface of the material increases, and gas molecules also gain enough energy to overcome the activation energy barrier, thus increasing the response. However, when the operating temperature exceeds the optimal reaction temperature, the desorption behavior of gas molecules may dominate, leading to a decrease in sensitivity [37,38]. The ZnO sensor has the strongest response signal at 240 °C, while the three ZnO/CDs sensors have the strongest response signal at 220 °C. What's more, the ZnO/CDs2 sensor has the strongest signal response to EG, while the response of the ZnO sensor to EG gas is lowest, indicating that the introduction of CDs can not only effectively reduce the optimal operating temperature (OOT) of the ZnO sensor, but also significantly enhances the signal response of the ZnO sensor. Due to its abundant oxygen vacancies and optimal recombination ratio, ZnO/CDs2 can generate the highest concentration of electrons and has the most effective electron transport channel, exhibiting the strongest response signal to EG. At the same time, Fig. 9b shows the relationship between baseline resistance in air and operating temperature of ZnO, ZnO/CDs1, ZnO/CDs2 and ZnO/CDs3 sensors. The baseline resistance of all four sensors decreases with the increase of the operating temperature, which indicates that above four samples have semiconductor properties. That means as the temperature increases, the carrier concentration increases, and the resistance decreases. It is also observed that the order of the baseline resistance is RZnO/CDs3 > RZnO/CDs2 > RZnO/CDs1 > RZnO. The resistance of samples with composite CDs is higher than that of pure ZnO. The reason is that due to the formation of p-n heterojunction between CDs and ZnO, in order to balance the Fermi energy level, electrons flow from the conduction band of ZnO to the conduction band of CDs, causing the electron depletion layer to become thicker and the resistance value to increase. Furthermore, the activation energy (Ea) of the four sensors were calculated, with the order of E
a(ZnO) > E
a(ZnO/CDs1) > E
a(ZnO/CDs3) > E
a(ZnO/CDs2) [39]. Among them, the Ea value of ZnO/CDs2 is the smallest, indicating that the sensing catalytic reaction is most likely to occur on its surface (Fig. S8). The transient resistance curves of the ZnO and ZnO/CDs sensors under OOT for 100 ppm EG are shown in Fig. 9c. The resistance of the sensors rapidly decreased under expose to the reducing gas EG, gradually converged to a stable value, and sharply recovered to the initial resistance after removal of the test gas. This is consistent with the characteristics of n-type MOS [40,41]. The response recovery curves of the four sensors are shown in Fig. 9d. The Ra/Rg values of ZnO, ZnO/CDs1, ZnO/CDs2, and ZnO/CDs3 are 69, 799, 1378, and 610, respectively. Compared with the ZnO sensor, the combination with CDs can significantly increase the sensitivity of ZnO/CDs2 sensor by 20 times. Moreover, the response/recovery times of ZnO, ZnO/CDs1, ZnO/CDs2, and ZnO/CDs3 were 79 s/22 s, 51 s/10 s, 45 s/9 s, and 59 s/15 s, respectively (Table S1). Compared with the ZnO sensor, the response/recovery time of the three ZnO/CDs sensors are generally shortened, indicating that the recombination of CDs can provide more convenient electron transport channels, promoting the gas adsorption and desorption. The ZnO/CDs2 sensor has the shortest response and recovery time, and also has the highest response to EG gas, exhibiting the best gas sensing performance. Therefore, subsequent gas sensing tests on ZnO/CDs2 were selected as the main research object. Fig. 9e shows the resistance variation of ZnO/CDs2 sensor with the relative humidity. As the humidity increases, the response of four sensors gradually decreases. This may be because water molecules occupy the active sites of material. Thus, in subsequent gas sensitivity tests, the relative humidity was fixed at 40 %.

Based on the previous analysis of the excellent photoelectric performance of ZnO/CDs2, the response of ZnO/CDs2 sensor to EG gas under different lighting conditions has been studied. Results are shown in Fig. 9f. The sensor shows a strong signal response under dark, infrared light (830–950 nm), visible light (400–750 nm), and UV light (<400 nm) irradiation, proving that the ZnO/CDs2 sensor can be suitable under various lighting conditions. Compared with only hot electrons under dark condition, ZnO/CDs2 can generate abundant photo-generated electrons after light excitation, and the response signal is significantly enhanced, which is consistent with the photocurrent test results. Especially, under UV light, the response of the ZnO/CDs2 sensor is strongest, reaching 2798, which is 15.8 times higher than under the dark condition. It is because the UV light energy is higher than the optical bandgap width of ZnO/CDs2, which can excite ZnO/CDs2 to generate a large quantity of photo-generated electrons and achieve the highest response signal to EG gas. As the excitation light wavelength shifts to visible light and then to infrared light, the incident light energy gradually weakens to a value smaller than the direct bandgap of ZnO/CDs2. Therefore, only a portion of photo-generated electrons are excited, showing a much smaller response to EG gas than that under UV light.

More specifically, the dynamic response recovery curves of ZnO/CDs2 under UV light irradiation for different concentrations of EG gas are shown in Fig. 10 a. Within the EG concentration range of 1–100 ppm, the response of ZnO/CDs2 exhibits a good linear relationship, as shown in Fig. 10b. According to the calculation method in a previous publication [42], the detection limit of ZnO/CDs2 under UV light is 0.021 ppm. Similarly, it can be observed that the ZnO sensor exhibits a significant linear relationship with the concentration of EG gas under UV light, with a detection limit of 0.019 ppm (Fig. 10c-d). In addition, the dynamic response curves and detection limits of ZnO/CDs2 and ZnO under visible light, infrared light, and dark conditions are also shown in Fig. S9. Under different lighting conditions, ZnO and ZnO/CDs2 exhibit good linear responses to different concentrations of EG gas. Obviously, the detection limits of ZnO/CDs2 and ZnO for EG are much lower than the 7.2 ppm guided by the American Society of Clinical Toxicology and the 4.33 ppm olfactory threshold in the human body. This shows impressively the practicality and universality of ZnO and ZnO/CDs2 as EG gas sensors under different lighting conditions. Compared with the response of ZnO to 100 ppm EG in dark, the signal of ZnO/CDs2 under UV light is increased by 100 times, and its detection limit is also reduced from 71 ppb to 21 ppb. The strong signal that is two orders of magnitude higher and the significantly reduced detection limit demonstrate that the introduction of CDs and light stimulation are effective ways to improve sensor performance (Figs. S9(i–j) and Fig. 10a-b). Specifically, the introduction of CDs increases the OV by 70 %, forming a heterojunction for electron transport, which can increase the response (Ra/Rg) of ZnO/CDs2 to 100 ppm EG from 27 to 167 (Figs. S9(i–l)). The UV light that best matches the ZnO/CDs2 band can enhance its response to 100 ppm EG from 167 to 2798 (Figs. S9(k–l) and Fig. 10a-b). Taking the ZnO/CDs2 and ZnO as examples, the logarithmic function of the response of two sensors to EG concentration is shown in Fig. S10. The previous studies have found that the power law index depends on the type of chemisorbed oxygen, which is a function of temperature [43]. The exponent value of ZnO/CDs2 and ZnO calculated are 0.92 and 0.78, respectively, indicating that the surface adsorbed oxygen is O−.

Selectivity is an important characteristic parameter of gas sensors [44]. In Fig. 10e, the sensitivity of ZnO and ZnO/CDs2 to 10 different VOCs under UV light was tested. The response of ZnO/CDs2 sensor to EG is much better than other VOCs, which is 71 times that of isopropanol. The response of ZnO to EG gas is 9 times that of formic acid. Both sensors show significant selectivity. The reason may be on the one hand that the dissociation bond energy of each gas is different, such as, EG (354.8 kJ/mol), ethanol (441.0 kJ/mol), methanol (440.2 kJ/mol), acetone (401.2 kJ/mol), ammonia (427.0 kJ/mol), N-butanol (551.2 kJ/mol), methanal (523.7 kJ/mol), N-propanol (400.1 kJ/mol), formate (493.0 kJ/mol) [37–42,45]. Among them, EG has the lowest bond dissociation energy. Therefore, the redox reaction is more likely to occur between EG gas and ZnO/CDs2, while other VOCs with high dissociation energy are relatively inert. On the other hand, due to the presence of two hydroxyl groups present in EG, it is easier to reduce, resulting in higher response in surface sensing reactions with adsorbed oxygen units [38].

Typical reported EG sensors have been investigated in detail and are listed in Table 1 for a more intuitive comparison of their gas sensing performance. Compared with those sensors, the ZnO/CDs2 sensor shows an attractive behavior for detecting EG gas, with ultra-high response, low ppb-level, and fast response rate. Meanwhile, a comparison of light-enhanced sensing performances using ZnO/CDs2 as sensing materials with other reported sensing materials is presented in Table 2 . Apparently, the ZnO/CDs2 sensor displays higher sensitivity and lower detection limit, whether under UV light, visible light, or infrared light irradiation.

Finally, the cyclic stability and long-term stability of ZnO/CDs2 sensor were tested. In Fig. 11 , under the excitation of different light sources, after five consecutive cycles, the resistance of sensor can still respond quickly and recover to the initial value, with little fluctuation in the response value. Afterwards, in a continuous stability test for half a month, the response values of the ZnO/CDs2 sensor maintained good stability under four lighting conditions, indicating that ZnO/CDs2 has stable performance and good practicality.

In fact, ZnO is an n-type semiconductor material with a Fermi level close to the conduction band. The CDs are p-type semiconductors, characterized by holes, with Fermi levels close to the valence band. When CDs are combined with ZnO, electrons diffuse from the high Fermi level to the low Fermi level, that is, electrons flow from the conduction band of ZnO to the conduction band of CD (Fig. 12 a). The ZnO/CDs still exhibit n-type semiconductor characteristics due to a small amount of CDs. The number of electron carriers in n-type semiconductors determines the resistance of the semiconductor materials. When the sensing material is exposed to high temperatures, oxygen molecules in the surrounding air can adsorb on its surface, producing adsorbed oxygen molecules. Then, these oxygen molecules are converted into surface adsorbed reactive oxygen species by taking free electrons from semiconductor materials. The reaction mechanism is illustrated as follows (Fig. 12b).
(3) O2(g) → O2(ads)
(4) O2(ads) + e− → O2 −(ads)
(5) O2 −(ads) + e− → 2O−(ads)
(6) O−(ads) + e− → O2 −(ads)
O2 −, O−, and O2− dominate at <100 °C, 100–300 °C and >300 °C, respectively [45]. Therefore, at 220–240 °C, the oxygen on the ZnO surface is mainly O−. During this process, the number of charge carriers in the ZnO/CDs that can participate in the conductivity process decreases and a thick electron-withdrawing layer is formed on the surface of the ZnO/CDs. When EG gas is poured into the test chamber, the O− on the surface of ZnO/CDs sensor reacted fast with it, as shown in the reaction equation (7). At this time, the electrons return to the conduction band and the resistance decreases (Fig. 9c).
(7) (CH2OH)2 + 5O−(ads) → 3H2O + 2CO2 + 5e−

More importantly, due to the effective excitation provided by light, ZnO/CDs generate a large number of photo-generated electrons and holes (Eq. (8)). The generation of photoelectrons can enhance the chemical adsorption of oxygen molecules and the ability of charge carriers to cross grain boundaries [49]. Meanwhile, CDs exhibit typical photo-induced electron transfer properties, which can transfer unstable photo-generated electrons onto the surface of ZnO, thereby suppressing electron hole recombination and greatly increasing electron concentration. The sharp increase in electron concentration will cause the response of the four sensors to EG under light illumination to be much higher than in darkness. Especially, the energy of UV light is most matched with the bandgap of the four samples, so their response under UV light is significantly higher than that under visible light and infrared light. The formation of p-n heterojunction is beneficial for the conversion of more O2 on the material surface, providing more O− (Eq. (9)) for the response process of EG, so the response of three ZnO/CDs sensors are better than ZnO. In addition, the generated
O
( hv )
‐
reacts with photo-generated holes (Eq. (10)), thus achieving the alternating cycle promotion of the photo-generated electron and hole reaction, thereby accelerating the separation of the photo-generated electron and hole.

In summary, ZnO/CDs were synthesized through a simple mechanical grinding method. Compared with ZnO, in ZnO/CDs2, the OV increased by 26 %, the photocurrent density increased 2.4 times, and the fluorescence emission intensity significantly decreased. Therefore, the response (Ra/Rg) of ZnO/CDs2 to 100 ppm EG gas under UV light, visible light, and infrared light is as high as 2798, 1378, and 467, respectively. Compared to the response of ZnO under UV light (Ra/Rg = 118), visible light (Ra/Rg = 69), and infrared light (Ra/Rg = 44), the signal of ZnO/CDs2 was enhanced by 23, 19, and 10 times, respectively. Especially, the detection limit of ZnO/CDs2 under UV light is as low as 21 ppb, and its response to 100 ppm EG is 71 times that of isopropanol, 192 times that of formic acid. In addition, ZnO/CDs2 also exhibit excellent cyclic and long-term stability. The reason may be that on the one hand, CDs have excellent light response ability, which can significantly increase the electron concentration of ZnO under light illumination. On the other hand, the PET property of CDs and the formation of p-n heterojunctions provide effective electron transport channels, avoiding a large amount of electron hole recombination. This work provides an outstanding method for manufacturing high-performance EG sensors and photo-excited sensors.
